title: Induced discommensurations in the lock-in transition of charge-density waves
abstract: We studied McMillan's free energy of the lock-in transition in charge-density waves. The wave profiles near the critical value were obtained by numerical annealing. First, we demonstrated that our method reproduces the previous studies. The obtained wave profiles include discommensurations near the critical value. Then, we calculated possible wave profiles in the commensurate state. We found that discommensurations are able to be excited in the commensurate state, leading the system to turn out to an incommensurate state. We proposed that these wave profiles result from topological invariants. Moreover, excitation of the discommensurations is favorable for the direction to the original wavelength of the incommensurate state. This is attributed to the nature of McMillan's free energy. The current-induced incommensurations, which we discovered with the diffraction study of $o$-TaS$_3$ [Inagaki \textit{et al.}, J. Phys. Soc. Jpn. 77, 093708 (2008)], is consistent with this study.
summary: This paper uses simulated annealing to study the lock-in transition in charge-density waves. It finds that discommensurations are topologically protected and can drive a commensurate system into an incommensurate state, providing a theoretical basis for observed current-induced incommensuration in materials like o-TaS3.
main_problem: Understanding the mechanism behind the emergence of discommensurations during the lock-in transition of charge-density waves (CDWs) and explaining current-induced incommensuration.
main_result: The study demonstrates that discommensurations are topologically protected and can be excited in the commensurate state, driving the system toward an incommensurate state, which is consistent with experimental observations in o-TaS3.
method: Numerical optimization using simulated annealing based on the Metropolis algorithm to minimize the McMillan free energy functional.
model_or_system: The model uses McMillan's free energy functional for charge-density waves in quasi-one-dimensional conductors like o-TaS3 and K0.3MoO3.
key_observables: CDW intensity, wave profiles, phase jumps, and free energy relative to the commensurate state.
important_parameters: The parameter Y (representing the third-order Umklapp process), the critical value Yc, and the phase theta.
main_assumptions: The model assumes a second-order transition via the McMillan free energy, though the authors note that the Umklapp term might induce a first-order transition.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the evolution of wave profiles during annealing; Figure 2 shows wave profiles for various Y values; Figure 3 shows profiles for different initial slopes; Figure 4 compares free energies of discommensurations and soliton-antisoliton pairs.
paper_structure: The paper begins with a validation of the numerical method, followed by an analysis of wave profiles near the critical value, an investigation of discommensurations in the commensurate state, a discussion of topological protection and energy asymmetry, and finally a comparison with experimental results.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Solitons.note: The paper directly studies the physics of commensurate-incommensurate transitions and the formation of discommensurations, which are types of topological solitons/kinks.
